title: The Customs Executive Order: Dealing with Downstream Consequences
gist: A new Trump executive order tightens U.S. customs enforcement by imposing far stricter requirements on importers of record, especially foreign ones, to combat tariff-driven fraud; while it should reduce fraud, it raises costs and complexity for legitimate importers and likely violates WTO national-treatment rules.

themes.theme: Tariffs create new incentives for customs fraud

insights.claim: Trump 2.0 tariffs that abandon most-favored-nation treatment and set different rates for different countries create stronger incentives to manipulate customs by transshipping goods through lower-tariff jurisdictions and falsifying country of origin.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Expect rising transshipment and origin-fraud, plus growing compliance scrutiny on import-heavy sectors and customs brokers.
insights.quote: In Trump 2.0, with MFN out the window and with potentially different tariff rates for different countries, if the courts ultimately approve his plans, incentives to manipulate the process are greater.

insights.claim: Higher U.S. tariff levels put more money on the table for evasion, since the average U.S. tariff before 2017 was less than 2.5 percent versus far higher current rates.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The size of the tariff gap is a direct proxy for fraud risk and enforcement intensity across trade lanes.
insights.quote: the average U.S. tariff before 2017 was less than 2.5 percent
insights.locator: The customs executive order will probably succeed

insights.claim: Trump's decisionmaking style is to act without considering downstream consequences and then create a patch to fix the problem he caused, as seen when first-term tariffs triggered retaliation that prompted a $23 billion taxpayer-funded farm bailout.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Policy patches signal recurring fiscal and regulatory whiplash; investors should anticipate sequential interventions rather than stable rules.
insights.quote: his response is to come up with a patch to fix the problem he caused
insights.locator: One of the characteristics of Trump

insights.claim: Chinese retaliation against Trump's tariffs in his second term is again hitting the farm community, with a similar bailout response expected.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Watch for new agricultural subsidy or bailout outlays as a fiscal and political tell on the tariff fight with China.
insights.quote: The same thing is happening in Trump 2.0, thanks to Chinese retaliation against his tariffs, and it looks like the response will be similar.
insights.locator: One of the characteristics of Trump

themes.theme: Stricter rules on importers of record

insights.claim: The customs executive order requires importers of record to disclose far more about themselves, including their chain of ownership and the extent of their U.S. assets, to stop importers with no U.S. assets from walking away from illegal transactions.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Compliance burden rises for all importers; firms reliant on opaque IOR structures face disruption.
insights.quote: IORs with no U.S. assets who simply walk away from illegal transactions, leaving CBP with no one to fine and no assets to seize
insights.locator: The customs executive order will probably succeed

insights.claim: Long-time trusted U.S. importers of record could suddenly be reclassified as foreign if they are part of an acquisition, subjecting them to tougher treatment.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: M&A involving import-dependent firms carries a hidden customs-status risk that could raise post-deal operating costs.
insights.quote: long-time trusted and reliable U.S. IORs will suddenly become foreign if they are part of an acquisition
insights.locator: The customs executive order will probably succeed

themes.theme: Higher costs for foreign importers via entries and bonds

insights.claim: Foreign importers of record will lose access to informal entries for low-value imports under $2,500 and must use the more demanding formal entry process, often requiring a licensed customs broker.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Customs brokerage demand and small-parcel import costs rise; a tailwind for compliance-services providers.
insights.quote: Foreign IORs will no longer be able to make informal entries, a process for lower-value imports under $2,500
insights.locator: The most significant distinctions relate to entries and bonds

insights.claim: Foreign importers of record will be barred from obtaining continuous annual bonds unless approved by CBP under the Customs Trade Partnership Against Terrorism, forcing them into costlier and slower single-entry bonds.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Bonding costs spike for small foreign importers who may lack collateral, squeezing thin-margin import businesses.
insights.quote: with higher tariffs, bonds are more expensive, posing an additional challenge for small importers who may not be able to come up with the necessary collateral
insights.locator: The most significant distinctions relate to entries and bonds

insights.claim: By treating foreign importers of record differently from U.S. ones, the customs executive order probably violates WTO national-treatment rules and could prompt other countries to take the United States to WTO dispute resolution.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Adds to the stack of pending trade-law challenges against the U.S., a slow-burn risk to trade-policy durability.
insights.quote: the order probably violates World Trade Organization (WTO) rules requiring national treatment

insights.claim: The order implements the punitive elements of long-discussed customs reform while ignoring the facilitation measures that would streamline entry for trusted importers, making importing harder and more expensive for legitimate businesses.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Compliant importers bear added cost with no offsetting facilitation; favors larger players able to absorb compliance overhead.
insights.quote: The Trump administration has characteristically chosen to implement the sticks and ignore the carrots.
insights.locator: Many of these ideas are not new
